coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot functionalized by chitosan and beta-cyclodextrin as a new support nanocomposite material for efficient methanol electrooxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Shadjou, Nasrin
Marandi, Maryam
coredata.dc:description: Current needs for sustainable energy have popularized the development and use of fuel cell technology. Catalysts are vital fuel cell components, and in recent years research efforts have focused on the development new types of catalysts. This work describes application of a novel nanocatalyst based on graphene quantum dot functionalized by chitosan (GQD-CS) and β-cyclodextrin (GQD-β-CD) towards electrooxidation of methanol in alkaline solution. The catalysts were prepared by cycling the glassy carbon electrode in solutions containing graphene quantum dot and chitosan. The synergistic effects and catalytic activity of the modified electrodes were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). It was found that, in the presence of methanol, the modified electrodes [(GQD-CS-GCE) and (GQD-β-CD-GCE)] exhibited a significantly higher catalytical response for methanol oxidation compared to the other samples. The anodic peak currents showed a linear dependency on the square root of the scan rate, which is a characteristic of a diffusion controlled process. The graphene quantum dots functionalized with chitosan and β-CD electrodes is further extended to the demonstration of novel electrocatalyst through the transfer of the electrode fabricated by bare GQDs. For comparison of the recognition efficiency, other electrodes including bare GCE, and GQDs-GCE were used for the control experiments. Comparison of recorded CVs and CHs in the presence of methanol using GQD-CS-GCE, β-CD-GQDs-GCE shows ΔEp was decreased as GQD-CS-GCE > GQDs-β-CD-GCE > GQDs-GCE. The higher peak current observed in the presence of GQD-CS-GCE, GQDs-β-CD-GCE clearly indicates β-CD and CS essential roles in the observed electrocatalytical behavior. In general, the attachment of chitosan and β-cyclodextrin to structure of GQDs provides new opportunities towards the direct methanol fuel cell application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalyst
Graphene quantum dot
Methanol
Energy storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQDs -ß-CD on the surface of GCE
Fabrication of GQD-CS on the surface of GCE
Chronoamperometric measurements of methanol at GQD-CS-GCE and GQD-β-CD-GCE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B) TEM and AFM image, (C, D) fluorescence spectra and DLS analysis of GQD.

(A) CVs of GCE (A), GQDs-GCE (B), GQDs-CS-GCE (C) in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 0.5 M methanol. (D) CVs of 0.5 M methanol at the surface of GCE, GQDs-GCE, and GQDs-CS-GCE. Supporting electrolyte: 0.1 M; NaOH; scan rate: 100 mV s−1.

(A) CVs of GCE (A), GQDs-GCE (B), GQDs-ß-CD-GCE (C) in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 0.5 M methanol. (D) CVs of 0.5 M methanol at the surface of GCE, GQDs-GCE, and GQDs-ß-CD-GCE. Supporting electrolyte: 0.1 M; NaOH; scan rate: 100 mV s−1.

A) CVs of 0.5 M methanol at the surface of GCE, GQDs-GCE, GQD-CS-GCE, and GQDs-ß-CD-GCE. Supporting electrolyte: 0.1 M; NaOH; scan rate: 100 mV s−1. B) Chronocoulometery of 0.5 M methanol at the surface of CS/GQDs/GCE, and GQDs-ß-CD-GCE. C) Nyquist plots for the GQD, GQD-CS-GCE, and GQD-β-CD-GCE electrode in NaOH solution (0.1 M) + 0.5 M methanol. Inset; Equivalent circuit compatible with the Nyquist diagrams for methanol electrooxidation on different GQD based electrodes.
Chronoamperograms of GQD-CS-GCE (A) GQDs-ß-CD-GCE (B) in 0.1 M NaOH in the presence of methanol at different potentials (1, 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, and 1.5 V), respectively.

(A) Cyclic voltammetry response of the GQD-CS-GCE in the alkaline solution at different scan rates (inner to outer) 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 90, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 50, 600, 700, 800, 900, and 1000 mV s−1.

(A) Cyclic voltammetry response of the GQDs-ß-CD-GCE in the alkaline solution at different scan rates (inner to outer) 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 90, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 50, 600, 700, 800, 900, and 1000 mV s−1.

The plots of peak currents vs. scan rates (A) and square root of scan rates (B) using GQD-CS-GCE. (C) The plot of peak potential separation vs. ln v. (D) The plot of lnIp vs. ln v. (E) The plot of lnIp vs. potential.

The plots of peak currents vs. scan rates (A) and square root of scan rates (B) using GQDs-ß-CD-GCE. (C) The plot of peak potential separation vs. ln v. (D) The plot of lnIp vs. ln v. (E) The plot of lnIp vs. potential.
CVs of GQD-CS-GCE (A) GQDs--CD-GCE (B) in different concentrations of methanol (0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.35, 0.4, 0.45, and 0.5 M). Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M of NaOH.

Chronoamperograms of GQD-CS-GCE (A) GQDs-ß-CD-GCE (B) in 0.1 M in the absence and presence (b-f of various concentrations of methanol: 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.45 and 0.5 M, respectively. Insets: Variation of chronoamperometry currents at vs. time −1/2 (b) and Icatal/Id vs. time 1/2 (c). Anodic currents obtained for a potential step of 1.5 V.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot functionalized by chitosan and beta-cyclodextrin as a new support nanocomposite material for efficient methanol electrooxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nano Technology Research Center, and Faculty of Science, Urmia University, Urmia 57154, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nano Technology Research Center, and Faculty of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Urmia University, Urmia 57154, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Current needs for sustainable energy have popularized the development and use of fuel cell technology. Catalysts are vital fuel cell components, and in recent years research efforts have focused on the development new types of catalysts. This work describes application of a novel nanocatalyst based on graphene quantum dot functionalized by chitosan (GQD-CS) and β-cyclodextrin (GQD-β-CD) towards electrooxidation of methanol in alkaline solution. The catalysts were prepared by cycling the glassy carbon electrode in solutions containing graphene quantum dot and chitosan. The synergistic effects and catalytic activity of the modified electrodes were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). It was found that, in the presence of methanol, the modified electrodes [(GQD-CS-GCE) and (GQD-β-CD-GCE)] exhibited a significantly higher catalytical response for methanol oxidation compared to the other samples. The anodic peak currents showed a linear dependency on the square root of the scan rate, which is a characteristic of a diffusion controlled process. The graphene quantum dots functionalized with chitosan and β-CD electrodes is further extended to the demonstration of novel electrocatalyst through the transfer of the electrode fabricated by bare GQDs. For comparison of the recognition efficiency, other electrodes including bare GCE, and GQDs-GCE were used for the control experiments. Comparison of recorded CVs and CHs in the presence of methanol using GQD-CS-GCE, β-CD-GQDs-GCE shows ΔEp was decreased as GQD-CS-GCE > GQDs-β-CD-GCE > GQDs-GCE. The higher peak current observed in the presence of GQD-CS-GCE, GQDs-β-CD-GCE clearly indicates β-CD and CS essential roles in the observed electrocatalytical behavior. In general, the attachment of chitosan and β-cyclodextrin to structure of GQDs provides new opportunities towards the direct methanol fuel cell application.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the ever-increasing use of energy, and especially of fossil fuels, is a major source of air pollution. As a result, there is a search for alternatives to fossil fuels, and fuel cells have attracted much attention due to their minimal pollutant emissions. Fuel cells are currently used in a wide range of portable, stationary and transport applications, and direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) are a particularly promising clean, portable power source. As such, DMFCs have received considerable interest with regard to applications in automobiles and portable consumer electronics [1] as well as other mobile and stationary devices [2,3]. This is because of their high efficiency, very low emissions and potential to act as renewable fuel sources with fast and convenient refueling, simple operation and ease of fuel storage and distribution. In addition, the low operating temperature of a DMFC (typically < 95 °C) allows for easy start-up and rapid responses to changes in the load or operating conditions [4]. A considerable number of studies have been devoted to the electrocatalytic oxidation of methanol, which is of paramount importance in the development of DMFCs [5]. One approach to the design of DMFCs is to use alkaline solutions [6], since there are many associated advantages, such as increased efficiency [6,7], a wider selection of possible electrode materials, almost no sensitivity to surface structures and negligible poisoning effects [8,9]. However, because the kinetics of methanol oxidation are generally unfavorable, a catalyst is required to improve the oxidation efficiency. Electrocatalysts based on Pt [10–12]. and Pt alloys [13–18] have been developed and used as anode catalysts for methanol oxidation in DMFCs [19], and have been shown to exhibit good activity for methanol oxidation. Unfortunately, these materials are too expensive for practical applications. Therefore, many attempts have been made to examine the catalytic activity of less expensive materials.

With the development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials have attracted substantial interest because of their unique optical, electrical, thermal and catalytic properties. These properties originating from quantum-size dimensions could change with their sizes and shapes. Since graphene was isolated from highly oriented pyrolytic graphite using the mechanical exfoliation method known as the “Scotch-tape method”, which was reported by Geim and Novoselov in 2004 [20], research into graphene's characteristic and potential application has grown exponentially due to its excellent mechanical, thermal, optical and electrical properties [21]. Recently, graphene-based nanomaterials such as graphene oxide (GO) [22], graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) [23] and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [24] have gained interest in various fields of research. GQD with tunable sizes, ie., 2.2 ± 0.3, 2.6 ± 0.2, and 3 ± 0.3 nm especially displays unique optical and electrical properties due to the quantum confinement effect [25]. GQD is a chemically inert, low toxicity and low cost nanomaterial compared to other nanomaterials. Moreover, it is a water-soluble nanocarbon [26]. Therefore, GQDs are attractive for use in analytical chemistry applications, particularly in spectroscopy [27–30] and electrochemistry [31].

Owing to the exciting properties such as high electrical conductivity, high surface area, tunable photoluminescence and excellent dispersion in various solvent, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as promising material for energy storage devices and fuel cells [32]. On the other hand, GQDs can be functionalized especially with oxygen containing groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups which can greatly enhance their hydrophilia and biocompatibility. One of important cases for this purpose is chitosan (CS). Chitosan is a linear polysaccharide composed of randomly distributed β-(1–4)-linked d-glucosamine (deacetylated unit) and N-acetyl-d-glucosamine (acetylated unit). It is made by treating shrimp and other crustacean shells with the alkali sodium hydroxide. CS has been gradually used for constructing catalysts due to its attractive properties that include excellent film-forming ability, high permeability, good adhesion, nontoxicity, cheapness and a susceptibility to chemical modification. It also facilitates the electron transfer after its swelling in the reaction mixture due to its hydrophilic nature [33,34]. Besides, chitosan can be used as a dispersant to form stable GQDs -chitosan composite which can form a stable film on the electrode surface. CS as a polysaccharide biopolymer, which displays excellent film-forming ability, high water permeability, good adhesion, and susceptibility to chemical modifications due to the presence of reactive amino and hydroxyl functional groups.

Other cases for functionalization of GQDs is cyclodextrin (CD.(CDs with their largely hydrophobic cavities of variable size and numerous ways of chemical modification are the subject of intensive electrochemical research including both their behavior in homogeneous solutions and in thin films attached to the electrode surfaces [35–37]. Therefore, CDs are employed in electrochemical devices and electrocatalysts for the direct electrooxidation. On the other hand, literature review show that, integration of CDs to the structure of electroactive materials such as graphene based materials can be enhancing their electrical conductivity [38]. These improved performances encouraged us to explore the possible leading role played by the presence of ß-CD/graphene or its conductive derivatives such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and functionalized GQDs. GQDs increase the contact area with the analyte, so they could increase the electrochemical active surface to interact with some electroactive analytes [39]. Since the increase in geometric surface area is very important parameter in electrocatalysis, therefore modification of different substrates (such as glass, carbon, graphite etc.) by GQDs can increase the rate of electrochemical reaction. Therefore, integration of ß-CD into GQDs can be provide by the zero-dimensional structure of the deposited films and greatly increases Faradic currents. Thus, in this paper, a nanocomposite of graphene quantum dot-β-cyclodextrin was fabricated on the surface of glassy carbon electrode by using one step electrodeposition method.

Excellent properties of GQDs and the advantages of CS and ß-CD have received more attention for preparation of high performance electrode materials [40]. It was for these reasons that we decided to explore the possibility of using the GQDs-CS system as a platform for the direct methanol fuel cell application. In this paper, GQDs-CS and GQDs- ß-CD were fabricated on the surface of glassy carbon electrode by using one step electrodeposition method. The redox behavior of prepared electrode was then characterized by cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The kinetics of charge transfer and mass transport processes across the interface were studied. In general, CS-GQDs show excellent electrocatalytic activities toward electrooxidation of methanol and great application potential in direct methanol fuel cell application.

All chemicals were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and used without further purification. Alumina slurry was purchased from Beuhler (Illinois, USA) and raw material of Methanol was purchased from Merck (Germany).

Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a three-electrode cell setup. The system was run on a Personal Computer using NOVA1.7 software. Saturated Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and the counter electrode (also known as auxiliary electrode), which usually made of an inert material was platinum. All potentials were measured with respect to the Ag/AgCl which was positioned as close to the working electrode as possible by means of a luggin capillary. Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (from Azar electrode Co., Urmia, Iran) was used as the working electrode. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on Leo 906, Zeiss, (Germany). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) experiments were performed at contact mode by Nanowizard AFM (JPK Instruments AG, Berlin, Germany) mounted on Olympus Invert Microscope IX81 (Olympus Co., Tokyo, Japan). UV–Vis spectroscopy was performed by Cecil, Cambridge, (UK). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using Siemens, D500, (Germany). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) were obtained using Malvern 3500 ZS. Spectrofluorimetery test was performed using Jasco, FP-750, (Tokyo, Japan).

An easy bottom-up method was used for the preparation of GQDs. At first, GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solutions [41]. Briefly, 2 g of citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 200 °C by a heating mantle until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, for preparing GQDs, 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution was added into the orange homogenous liquid dropwise with continuous stirring. The obtained GQD solution was stable for at least one month at 4 °C.

Fig. 1 (A and B) presents the AFM and TEM images of synthesized GQDs. The corresponding AFM image shows a single GQD monolayer thin film. Ninety percent of the particles represent dark brown color which assigned to a size range below 10 nm. Furthermore, the DLS study represented hydrodynamic sizes of GQDs with size distribution of 5 ± 4 nm (Fig. 1C) which findings of DLS analysis confirmed the AFM results.

Fig. 1D shows fluorescence spectra of the GQD dispersed in water at room temperature. The GQDs have a broad absorption, from 400 to 600 nm. The maximum excitation of 210 nm was obtained with an emission wavelength of ∼470 nm. The maximum emission of ∼480 nm was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Also when the excitation wavelength changed from 340 to 420 nm, the maximum peaks were constant. This could be explained by the uniformity both in the size and the surface state of those sp2 clusters contained in GQD which was responsible for the fluorescence of GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs -ß-CD on the surface of GCE
ce:section.ce:para: GCE (2 mm in diameter) was polished to a mirror-like finish with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry and then thoroughly rinsed with double distilled water. Then it was successively sonicated in acetone and double distilled water and was allowed to dry at room temperature. Finally, 5 mL homogenous GQD and GQDs-ß-CD films were electrodeposited onto GCE by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential range from −1.0–1.0 V at a scan rate of 200 mV s-1 for 50 cycles (Scheme 1 ). After 50 repeating cycles, the three-electrode system was transferred into 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution containing analytes and cyclic voltamograms were recorded at a sweep rate of 100 mV s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQD-CS on the surface of GCE
ce:section.ce:para: GCE (2 mm in diameter) was polished to a mirror-like finish with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry and then thoroughly rinsed with double distilled water. Then it was successively sonicated in acetone and double distilled water and was allowed to dry at room temperature. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out for the electrodeposition of GQDs on GCE (3 mm diameter) from GQDs aqueous solution (2 g/L) in the potential range from 0 to 1.0 V at a scan rate of 100 mV−1. After 60 repeating cycles, the three-electrode system was transferred into 0.1 M HCl containing 2 g L−1 CS, and 20 repetitive cycle in the potential range from −0.1−1.5 V at a scan rate of 200 mV−1. Finally, thin film of GQDs-CS was obtained at the surface of GCE (Scheme 2 ).

Cyclic voltammetry was used for investigation electrocatalytical activity of the GCE, GQD-GCE and GQD-CS-GCE for electrooxidation of methanol. Fig. 2 A shows CVs of bare GCE, GQD-GCE and GQD-CS-GCE in the absence and presence of methanol in 0.1 M alkaline solution. On the bare GCE there is no redox peaks appeared on the absence of analyte. But, using GQD-GCE as well as GQDs-ß-CD-GCE, and GQD-CS-GCE there is an anodic peak in the absence of methanol. Although the peak currents are remarkably increased at the GQDs-GCE due to the excellent electrical conductivity of GQDs, the Ipox is 0.29 mA which is still too small (Fig. 2B). When CS as catalyst agent is used to modify GQD-GCE, the differences in both peak current is obviously enlarged and reach to 1.1 mA (Fig. 2C), suggesting that GQD-CS can provide an acceleration center to electron shuttling. More importantly, the lowering of the overvoltage (0.057 V) observed in the presence of GQD clearly indicates its essential role in the observed electrocatalytical behavior. These results indicated that GQDs-CS film could accelerate the rate of electron transfer of methanol and have good electrocatalytical activity for redox reaction of methanol. Therefore, GQD-CS is a suitable mediator to shuttle electron between methanol and working electrode, and facilitate electrochemical regeneration following electron exchange with methanol. This observation is also linked to the high conductivity and inherent ability of GQD-CS. That might be related to the excellent properties of GQD-CS such as high specific surface area and electrical conductivity. It is no doubt that CS play important roles in the improvement of catalytically activity of GQDs towards electrooxidation of methanol. As a decisive catalytically component in the composite film, GQD-CS provides a catalytically platform for selective detection of methanol meanwhile, GQDs can amplify the electrochemical signals produced during the electrochemical sensing process. In one word, the synergetic effect of GQDs and CS leads to a successful and effective oxidation of methanol. On the other hand, comparison of recorded CVs in the presence of methanol using GQD-CS-GCE, GQDs-GCE, and GCE shows ΔEp was decreased as GQD-CS-GCE > GQDs-GCE ≫ GCE (Fig. 2D). The larger lowering of the overvoltage (0.057 V) observed in the presence of CS-GQDs-GCE clearly indicates its essential role in the observed electrocatalytical behavior. This observation is also linked to the high conductivity and inherent ability of GQD-CS. That might be related to the excellent properties of GQD-CS such as high specific surface area and electrical conductivity. It is no doubt that CS play important roles in the improvement of catalytically activity of GQDs towards electrooxidation of methanol. As a decisive catalytically component in the composite film, GQD-CS provides a catalytically platform for sensitive detection of methanol, meanwhile, GQDs can amplify the electrochemical signals produced during the electrochemical sensing process. In one word, the synergetic effect of GQDs and CS leads to a successful and effective electrocatalytical oxidation methanol.

Fig. 3 (A–C) presents the CVs of GQDs-ß-CD-GCE, both in the absence (a) and in the presence (b) of methanol in alkaline solution along with GQD/GCE and GCE. At GQDs-ß-CD-GCE (Fig. 3A), oxidation of methanol resulted in a typical electrocatalytic response. In the presence of methanol, it was observed that the anodic current and the associated anodic charge increased drastically. The results indicated that methanol was oxidized by an active ß-CD moiety through a cyclic mediation redox process.

The significant enhancement of the methanol electrooxidation at ß-CD-GQD/GCE can be explained according to the catalysis mechanism. Because the GQD had a high surface area, it boosted the faradic currents of the sluggish reaction as well as increased the amount of chemisorbed layer of the methanol. This behavior catalyzed the electron transfer of Methanol in the solution. The more active functional groups on the surface of the GQDs-ß-CD can easily form hydrogen bonds with the OH groups of the methanol, which weaken the OH bond energies; the electrons would be transferred through OH⋯HO. In addition, the density of the electron follows as methanol > intermediate > product and therefore, intermediate and product electroactivity decreased and they were difficultly oxidized which is in accordance with the experimental results. Also, the electrocatalytic effect of ß-CD-GQD can be ascribed to the combination of the electron-acceptor ability of GQD confinement effects due to the attachment to the ß-CD. On the basis of the previous considerations, the electrocatalytic effect of GQDs-ß-CD must result from the superposition of the effect associated with more external OH units and that due to boundary-associated ones. On the other hand, since the catalytic effect associated with GQDs-ß-CD consists essentially of an enhancement of the anodic peak for methanol oxidation, the catalytic effect can tentatively be associated with a redox reaction in solution phase between OH units and any intermediate species resulting from the initial electron transfer of methanol (Fig. 3D).

The electrocatalytical behavior of the GQD-ß-CD-GCE, GQD-CS-GCE, and GQD-GCE were evaluated by employing CV as electrochemical techniques. Fig. 4 A display CVs for methanol at alkaline solution using GQD-ß-CD-GCE, GQD-CS-GCE, and GQD-GCE along with the CVs of GCE (a). As seen in Fig. 4, I GQD-ß-CD-GCE
/I GQD-CS-GCE for electrooxidation of methanol is 1.61. In addition, I GQD-ß-CD-GCE
/I GQD-GCE and I GQD-CS-GCE
/I GQD-GCE are 0.7 and 1.0, respectively. The data obtained clearly show that the combination of CS with GQDs definitely improve the characteristics of methanol oxidation which was compare with ß-CD. Therefore methanol could be more accumulated on the electrode surface due to their interaction with ß-CD. Therefore, for these two modified electrodes the value of the oxidation peak current follows the order of Ip [GQDs-CS-GCE] > Ip [-GQDs-ß-CD-GCE] > Ip [GQDs-GCE]. Literature information reveals that the increased anodic signal may also be a result of its inclusion into the hydrophobic cavity of ß-CD. Because the GQD and CS had a high surface area and functional group, respectively, GQDs-CS-GCE boosted the faradic currents of the sluggish reaction as well as increased the amount of chemisorbed layer of the methanol. This behavior catalyzed the electron transfer of methanol in the solution. Enlargement of current to 1.1 mA and 0.7 mA using GQD-ß-CD-GCE and GQD-CS-GCE suggesting that GQD-ß-CD and GQD-CS can provide acceleration centers to electron shuttling. Compartment the obtained CVs illustrated that, CS has more effective role on electrocatalytical behavior of prepared GQD based catalysts towards detection of methanol. Finally, it is recognized that functional groups post modification elements of GQD can change the oxidation potential of analytes. Therefore, change on the oxidation potential of the CV, resulting from the electrochemical interaction of methanol with surface of modified GQDs, can be extremely useful for providing reliable chemical information about electroactivity of analyte. Also, it can be seen that amino acids with the same functional groups have similar oxidation potentials. Also, compartment of peak currents show that methanol has greater interaction with GQD-CS-GCE when compare with GQDs-ß-CD-GCE. Hence higher peak current of analytes is expected because of existence of accessible OH groups at the surface of CS. Therefore, the reason of this phenomenon is difference of functional groups of CS and β-CD in GQD based electrodes. In Fig. 4A, the anodic current of the electro-oxidation of methanol with the modified electrode indicates a pronounced electrocatalytic effect. Furthermore, superior results were obtained when applying a GQD-CS-GCE or GQDs-ß-CD-GCE compared with the GQD-GCE. In addition, it is important to point out, the surface average concentration of electroactive (moL/cm2) GQD-CS and GQDs-ß-CD on the GCE can be calculated from the charge integration of the anodic peak in the cyclic voltammogram according to the formula, Q = nFA, where Q is the charge consumed in C, A is the electrode area (cm2), F is the Faraday constant, and n is the number of electrons transferred (see Fig. 4B). The electroactive CS-GQD concentration on the GCE is estimated to be 2.13 × 10−8 mol/cm2, which is one orders of magnitude higher than GQDs-ß-CD (and 4.12 × 10−9 mol/cm2), indicating saturated adsorption of CS and ß-CD in prepared GQDs film.

To verify these results, the Nyquist diagrams of GQD-ß-CD-GCE, GQD-CS-GCE, and GQD/GCE were acquired in methanol (0.5 mol/L) at a DC offset potential equal to that of the corresponding methanol oxidation peak of each sample. From Fig. 4C, it is evident that the pure GQDs showed the highest resistance values together with the lowest current densities, while the GQD-ß-CD-GCE, GQD-CS-GCE exhibited the lowest resistance and highest current density. These data are in good agreement with the CVs. The equivalent circuit compatible with the Nyquist diagram recorded in the presence of methanol is presented in the inset of Fig. 4C. To obtain a satisfactory impedance simulation of methanol electro-oxidation, it is necessary to replace the capacitor C with a constant phase element (CPE), Q, in the equivalent circuit. The most widely accepted explanation for the presence of CPE behavior and depressed semicircles on solid electrodes is microscopic roughness causing an inhomogeneous distribution in the solution resistance as well as in the double-layer capacitance. In this electrical equivalent circuit, Rs, CPEdl and Rct represent solution resistance, a constant phase element corresponding to the double layer capacitance and the charge transfer resistance. CPEads and Rads are the electrical elements related to the adsorption of reaction intermediates. In this circuit the charge transfer resistance of the electrode reaction has a simple physical meaning indicating how fast charge transfer occurs during methanol electrooxidation at the surface of the electrode.

The above analysis for the electrocatalytical oxidation of methanol is further supported by chronoamperometric measurements indicated in Fig. 5 . The potentials applied are 0.53 and 0.71 V vs. Ag/AgCl which are close to those of anodic peaks of methanol at the surface of GQD-ß-CD-GCE and GQD-CS-GCE, respectively. Fig. 5 shows that compared in the presence of CS on the structure of GQD, much large increase of the current observed than that GQD-ß-CD. This is consistent with the obtained results by EIS and CV techniques. According to these results, methanol adsorbs to the GQD-ß-CD and GQD-CS, due to electrostatic interactions with protonated amine groups within the film.

Scan rate is one of parameters significantly affecting electrooxidation of various compounds. Also, useful information involving electrochemical mechanism usually can be acquired from the relationship between peak current and scan rate. Therefore, the electrochemical behaviors of methanol at different scan rates were investigated on the surface of GQD-ß-CD-GCE, GQD-CS-GCE by CV (Figs. 6 and 7
). The cyclic voltammetric response of the modified electrode had the expected wave shape for a surface-confined redox center which is dependent of the potential scan rate for sweeping rates from 2 to 1000 mV/s (Fig. 8 A). In addition, the current was directly proportional to the rate of potential scan over the range from 20 to 700 mV/s, suggesting facile charge transfer between the electrodeposited GQD-CS and the GCE (Figs. 6 and 7). This behavior leads to changing of the large enhancement of the anodic current and complete disappearance of cathodic peak current. Also, Fig. 9 A shows the cyclic voltammograms of methanol solution at different sweep rates. A linear relationship was obtained between the peak current and the scan rate in the range and 20–150 mV/s, which revealed that the oxidation of methanol was an adsorption-controlled step. Two approaches widely used to study the reversibility of reactions and to determine whether a reaction is adsorption or diffusion controlled consist of the analyses of dependences: Ip vs v 1/2 and ln Ip vs ln v. Figs. 8(B and C) and 9 (BC) shows these plots for the oxidation peak of methanol in 0.1 M alkaline solution. For reversible or irreversible systems without kinetic complications, Ip varies linearly with v 1/2, intercepting the origin. Although, the plot of Ip on v 1/2 presented in Figs. 8B and 9B is linear (R2 = 0.9996), it does not cross the origin of the axes. This is characteristic for the electrodic process preceded by electrochemical reaction and followed by a homogenous chemical reaction. In the scan rate range from 10 to 200 mV−1, peak current (Ip) of methanol electrooxidation depends linearly on square root of the scan rate (v) and is described by the following equation:
Ip GQD-CS-GCE = 0.0745 v (V1/2 s−1)mA + 0.1262 mA (R2 = 0.9968)
Ip GQD-ß-CD-GCE = 0.1 v (V1/2 s−1)mA + 0.1314 mA (R2 = 0.9927)

This dependence does not cross the origin. This fact can suggest that the electrode process of methanol electrooxidation is controlled by diffusion. On the other hand, a dependence of ln Ip on ln v (Figs. 8C and 9C) is linear and described by the following equation:
ln Ip GQD-ß-CD-GCE = {0.5603ln v (V s−1)}−7653 mA (R2 = 0.9946)
ln Ip GQDCS-GCE = {0.2222ln v (V s−1)}−0.9523 mA (R2 = 0.9867)

Its slope is 0.56 and 0.22 and indicates adsorption-control of the electrode process. A slope close to 0.5 is expected for diffusion-controlled electrode processes and close to 1.0 for adsorption-controlled processes [42–45].

Figs. 8D and 9D presents a dependence of Ep on scan rate determined from cyclic voltammograms recorded for the methanol electrooxidation. If electrochemical reaction is irreversible, then Ep is independent on v. Thus, it can be concluded that heterogeneous electron transfer in methanol electrooxidation is irreversible because Ep increases with an increase in the scan rate. In addition, the value of the overall electron transfer coefficient for the reaction can be obtained from the following equation [46,47]:
E p
=
(
R T
2 β n F
)
L n v + c o n s
tan
t
where Ep-peak potential (V), R-universal gas constant (8.314 J K−1 mol−1), F-Faraday constant (96,487 C mol−1), T-Kelvin temperature (298 K), βn -anodic transfer coefficient, v-scan rate (V s−1).

Using the dependence of anodic peak potential on the logarithm of the potential scan rate (Figs. 8D and 9D), the value of the overall electron transfer coefficient (βn) was obtained as 0.44 and 0.50 for methanol electrooxidation at the GQD-CS-GCE and GQD-ß-CD-GCE, respectively.

It can also be noted in Figs. 5 and 6 that with increasing the sweep rate, the peak potential for the catalytic oxidation of methanol shifts to more positive potentials, suggesting a kinetic limitation in the reaction between the redox sites of the GQD-CS and methanol. However, the oxidation currents for methanol increase linearly with the square root of the sweep rate (Figs. 8B and 9B), suggesting that at sufficient overpotential the reaction is mass transport controlled. Also, a plot of the sweep rate-normalized current versus sweep rate, exhibits the characteristic shape typical of an ECcat. process. These results show that the overall electrochemical oxidation of methanol at modified electrode might be controlled by the cross-exchange process between methanol and the redox site of the GQD-CS film and the diffusion of methanol.
In order to obtain information on the rate-determining step of GQD-ß-CD-GCE, GQD-CS-GCE, Tafel slope (b = RT/βn F) was determined as 0.63 V and 0.7 mV, respectively (Figs. 8E and 9E).

According to Bard and Faulkner [44], current-potential characteristic of the oxidation irreversible process is described by the following equation:
I p
= 0.227 F A C
[
exp
( β n f )
(
E p
)
]
where Ip-peak current (A), f = F/RT, A-electrode area (cm2), C-bulk concentration of methanol (mol cm−3), and. This equation allowed obtaining dependence:
L n
I p
= L n a −
( β n f )
E p

A dependence of ln (Ip) vs. (Ep) is linear (Figs. 8E and 9E) and described by the following equation:
ln Ip GQD-CS-GCE = {1.9315 [(Ep)V]} mA−2.4244 mA (R2 = 0.9849)
ln Ip GQD-ß-CD-GCE = {2.511 [(Ep)V]} mA−5.3121 mA (R2 = 0.9840)
and its slope is 1.9315 and 2.511 anodic transfer coefficient (βn) of methanol electrooxidation at the surface of GQD-ß-CD-GCE, GQD-CS-GCE. This result is close to obtained from previous method (Ep vs. ln v). This slop indicates also the three electron transfer to be rate-limiting, assuming a charge transfer coefficient of β = 0.6 and 0.72, respectively.

Fig. 10 A and B summarizes the behavior of the modified electrode in NaOH solution (0.1 mol/L) in the presence of various concentrations of methanol at a scan rate of 100 mV/s. In this figure, the growth of the anodic peak is considerable and the negligible cathodic peak in the presence of methanol is attributed to an irreversible oxidation process. It can be seen that the anodic peak current density increases with increasing methanol concentration, but plateaus at a concentration of 1.5 M. In this process, methanol molecules adsorbed on the electrode surface are oxidized, decreasing the number of sites available for subsequent adsorption. Eventually, the overall rate of methanol oxidation decreases and the anodic current density remains constant due to saturation of the surface sites. Higher methanol concentrations, thus lead to more pronounced passivation of the electrode surface. The oxidation of methanol involves both the adsorption of reactant/intermediate species or methanol oxidation products and the formation of a passive, nonconductive layer of oligomeric products resulting from the oxidation process on the electrode surface. In the reverse half cycle, the oxidation continues and the corresponding current density goes through a maximum due to the regeneration of active sites for the adsorption of methanol. It is important to note that the modified electrode functions as a stable electrocatalysts because the current density from the electro-oxidation of methanol was found to be almost constant over 46 cycles. This result indicates that methanol reacts at the electrode surface without generating a poisoning effect. In fact, the modified electrode was found to function over the course of several weeks without the need to renew the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chronoamperometric measurements of methanol at GQD-CS-GCE and GQD-β-CD-GCE
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the reaction kinetics, the electrocatalytical oxidation of methanol onGQD-CS-GCE was investigated by chronoamperometry. Chronoamperometry, as well as cyclic voltammetry has been employed for the investigation of the processes occurring via an ErCi mechanism [44]. Single steps chronoamperograms were recorded by setting the working electrode potentials to desired values and were used to measure the catalytic rate constant on the modified surface. GQD-CS-GCE and GQD-β-CD-GCE was done by setting the working electrode potential at 1.5 V and for increasing concentrations of methanol. It is evident that there is an increase in the fixed time current plots for increasing concentrations of methanol. Fig. 11 A and B shows a plot of the fixed time current, the current measured after 25s, as a function of methanol concentration in a 0.1 M alkaline solution.

For an electroactive material with a diffusion coefficient, D, the current for the electrochemical reaction (under diffusion control) is described by the Cottrell equation [44]:
I = n F A
D
1 / 2
C ∗
π
− 1 / 2
t
− 1 / 2
where D and C are the diffusion coefficient (cm2/s) and the bulk concentration (mol/cm3), respectively. The plot of I versus t0.5 will be linear (Fig. 11 Ab and Bb), and from the slope, the value of diffusion coefficient can be obtained. A mean value of 0.5 × 10−5 cm2/s and 1.1 × 10−5 cm2/s was calculated for D using the slopes of I-t0.5 plots of methanol with GQD-ß-CD-GCE and GQD-CS-GCE, respectively. Also, the rate constant for the following chemical reaction, k'h, (the chemical reaction between methanol and the surface-confined mediator) can be evaluated by chronoamperometry according to the method described in Ref. [44]:
I
c a t a l
I d
=
λ
1 / 2
[
π
1 / 2
e r f
(
λ
1 / 2
)
+
exp
( − λ )
λ
1 / 2
]
where I
catal is the catalytic current in the presence of methanol, Id the limiting current in the absence of methanol and λ = kC
m
t (k, C
m and t are the catalytic rate constant, bulk concentration of methanol and the elapsed time, respectively) is the argument of the error function. For λ > 1.5, erf (λ
1/2
) almost equals unity and Eq. (3) reduces to:
I
c a t a l
I d
=
λ
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
=
π
1 / 2
(
k
C m
t
)
1 / 2

The above equation can be used to calculate the rate constant of the catalytic process, kh. Having measured the catalytic current, i.e., Icatal, it is possible to carry out the electrode process in identical conditions, but in the absence of methanol, in order to determine Id. Knowing the ratio Icatal/Id, one can simply calculate the value of kh for a given concentration of substrate from the slope of ICatal/Id versus t0.5 plot (Fig. 11 Ac and Bc). Such a plot was constructed from the chronoamperograms of a GQD-ß-CD-GCE and GQD-CS-GCE in the presence of methanol. In these conditions, the gas formation has no effect on the catalytic process and the electron transfer between methanol and the superficial sites of modifier is the sole rate determining factor. The calculated value of kh is 2.01 × 105 and 4.11 × 105 (M s)−1 using the slope of Icatal = Id/t0.5 curve. This value of kh explain well the sharp feature of the catalytic peak observed for catalytic oxidation of methanol at the GQD-CS-GCE and GQD-ß-CD-GCE, respectively.

In conclusion, the work presented describes a novel thin film for the manufacture of methanol fuel cell electrocatalystes. In summary, thin film of GQD-β-CD and GQDs-CS were formed electrochemically in a regime of cyclic voltammetry on a glassy carbon electrode and checked for electrooxidation of methanol in alkaline solution. Catalytical efficiency of as GQD-β-CD-GCE, CS-GQD-GCE, and GQD-GCE was examined using CV, CA, and EIS. The results indicates β-CD and CS essential role in the observed electrocatalytical behavior of GQDs. Comparison of recorded cyclic voltammograms in the presence of methanol using GQD-β-CD-GCE, GQDs-CS-GCE, and GQD-GCE shows GQDs-CS-GCE has benefit for this purpose than GQDs-β-CD-GCE and GQD-GCE. It is expected that this work opens new horizons on the design of advanced nano-catalysts based on GQDs towards the direct methanol fuel cell application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the partial financial support by the Drug Applied Research Center Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences and Nano Technology Research Center, and Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University.
